protocol: Five free-text prompts surfaced during session 3 (after card-sort + pairwise + three-layer framings have already given the user the vocabulary). Each prompt asks for one specific concrete memory or anticipation; 30–120 words is the target response length. The user can skip any prompt; declining is itself a signal.
coding_strategy: LLM-coded post-hoc into (domain, value_id) tags with confidence scores; validation-cohort responses are human-verified by a second coder. Inter-rater reliability target κ ≥ 0.70 before LLM coding is trusted at scale. Coding extracts: which value(s) the story touches; which signature (cost-of-virtue, consistency, in-group/out-group, etc.); valence (proud / regret / aspirational / neutral).
design_intent: Free text complements forced-choice. Pairwise tells us what the user picks when given options; story prompts tell us what they SURFACE unprompted. Spontaneous content is often more predictive than checked-list content. The five prompts span four temporal/evaluative frames (recent proud / recent regret / anticipated test / admired model / aspirational target) so the qualitative layer triangulates the quantitative one.

prompts.text: Tell me about a time, recently, when you were quietly proud of how you handled something. Not a big moment; one of the small ones. Two or three sentences is plenty.
prompts.follow_up: If you can: what was the alternative — what would the less-proud version of you have done in that moment?

prompts.framing: Recent moral self-reflection — let yourself down
prompts.text: Tell me about a time, recently, when you let yourself down — not catastrophically, just a small moment when you knew you didn't act like the person you want to be.
prompts.follow_up: What would the version of you you'd respect have done instead?

prompts.text: Is there a situation coming up — this week or this month — that you think will test you in some way? Just describe it briefly; you don't have to know what you'll do.

prompts.framing: Someone you respect — what they do
prompts.text: Earlier you brought to mind someone you deeply respect. Tell me one specific thing they do — a behavior, a habit, a moment you witnessed — that earns that respect.
prompts.follow_up: Do you do that thing yourself?

prompts.framing: Who you want to become — one specific behavior
prompts.text: Imagine the version of yourself you'd most like to be in five years — not in achievements, in character. Name one specific way they'd act that you don't, yet.
prompts.follow_up: What gets in the way today?

ongoing_story_capture.rationale: After the session-3 batch, story prompts return on a slower cadence — roughly one every 5–7 sessions — to track drift and capture new context.
ongoing_story_capture.rotation_strategy: Cycle through the five prompts in randomized order; never repeat a prompt within a 14-session window.

what_we_do_not_do: We do not ask 'what is the most ethical thing you've ever done?' or any other prompt that invites narrative inflation. Specificity + temporal proximity (recent / upcoming) is the consistent anti-inflation move.
We do not store the free text in cleartext on the server. The user's local-first store holds the original; only the LLM-extracted tag tuples sync (with consent).

We do not surface user stories back to the user in summarized form during MVP-1. The temptation to do 'here's what you wrote about yourself last month' is real but introduces a feedback loop the validation study can't control for. Defer to MVP-2.
